title: git said the feature shipped
description: A commit is halfway: software ships when you watch a customer complete the intended action live.
tldr: Git said the feature shipped. The customer’s screen said otherwise. A commit is halfway because shipping ends when a user can actually do the thing.

sections.h2: when is a feature actually shipped?
body: A feature is shipped when a user is doing the thing it was built for.
Merged code, green CI, and a closed ticket can still leave the customer unable to use it.

sections.h2: why does repo activity feel like progress?
body: Repo activity produces visible signals: commits, merges, CI, and ticket movement.
Those signals describe work moving through the repository. They do not show whether the customer’s screen works.

sections.h2: what did the customer screen reveal that Git missed?
body: Git said the feature shipped. The customer’s screen said otherwise.
That gap is the receipt: repository status cannot stand in for watching the feature work live.

sections.h2: what should count as done on a team?
body: Done should include seeing the intended user action work live.
A commit is halfway. The remaining half is the customer experience.

faqs.q: Does green CI mean a feature is shipped?
faqs.a: Green CI shows that CI is green. It does not show that a customer can complete the intended action on their screen.

faqs.q: Is a merged commit considered done?
faqs.a: A merged commit is halfway. The work reaches done when a user is doing the thing the feature was meant to enable.

faqs.q: Why can a closed ticket still hide a shipping problem?
faqs.a: Tickets can close after repository work is complete. The customer’s screen can still reveal that the feature does not work live.
